entries.adjudication_notes: No caller passes root, but all 6 callers are tests and the adapter is staged unwired code; production-wired sibling passes root - parameter becomes load-bearing at wiring time. Removal is churn. BORDERLINE: staged-code timing case.

finding.contract_claim: L105: parameter `finding_from_junk.root` — All 6 call sites invoke `finding_from_junk` with only a `JunkFinding` positional argument (via `_junk(...)`) and never pass `root` as a keyword or positional argument. The parameter always takes its default value of `None`. The `root` value is forwarded to `_norm_path`, where the branch `if root is not None and p.is_absolute()` (inside `_norm_path`) handles it, but that gating is in the helper — no conditional branch gated exclusively by `root` exists inside `finding_from_junk` itself.
finding.observed_behavior: Remove the `root` keyword parameter from `finding_from_junk` and replace the call `_norm_path(jf.source_path, root)` with `_norm_path(jf.source_path)` (passing `None` implicitly via `_norm_path`'s own default). If `root`-based path relativization is ever needed in the future it can be re-added then.

entries.adjudication_notes: Nobody - not even tests - injects executor; docstring declares injection intent for provider only. Unexercised DI seam on a central class. BORDERLINE: defensible either way (DI affordance vs YAGNI).
evidence_consulted: checked test fixtures use real executors

finding.contract_claim: L162: parameter `Triage.__init__.executor` — None of the 16 deduplicated call sites pass `executor`. Every caller either passes no optional args (call site 1), passes only `rate_limiter` (call site 2), or passes only `provider` (call sites 5–16). The parameter's only use in the body is the inline expression `executor or ExplorationExecutor(config)`, which always resolves to `ExplorationExecutor(config)` because `executor` is always `None`.
finding.observed_behavior: Remove the `executor` parameter and replace the assignment with `self.executor = ExplorationExecutor(config)` unconditionally. No full if-block is gated — only the inline `or` expression simplifies.

entries.adjudication_notes: Never passed, always 200, but parametrizes a live truncation branch and mirrors test-exercised sibling tunables; removing hurts testability. BORDERLINE: significance-based dismissal.

finding.contract_claim: L40: parameter `ExplorationExecutor.__init__.max_list_entries` — None of the 16 call sites pass `max_list_entries`. The production call site (triage.py:172) uses `ExplorationExecutor(config)` with no optional args, and all 14 test call sites either pass no optional args or pass only `max_file_bytes` or `max_grep_matches`. The parameter always takes its default value of 200.
finding.observed_behavior: Consider hardcoding 200 as a module-level constant (e.g. `_MAX_LIST_ENTRIES = 200`) and removing the constructor parameter, or leave it as a tunable but document that no caller currently overrides it. No conditional branches are exclusively gated by this parameter — `self.max_list_entries` is used as a loop cap threshold that always runs, not as a guard for an optional code path.

entries.adjudication_notes: [carried from audit-dead_code-000 r1] Sentinel filter in async_utils gather never removes anything (control-flow proof); silently dropping sentinel would mask bugs; cleanup fix.

finding.contract_claim: L54-54: The `_SENTINEL` filter `[result for result in results if result is not _SENTINEL]` is never triggered in practice. In the success path all slots are filled; in the exception + `return_exceptions=False` path the function raises before reaching this line; in the `return_exceptions=True` path exceptions are stored directly so all slots are also filled. The filter is an always-no-op guard.
finding.observed_behavior: Replace with a plain `return list(results)` (after casting) or add an assertion `assert _SENTINEL not in results` for debugging. If the sentinel guard is intentionally defensive, document why residual sentinels could occur.

entries.adjudication_notes: Provably unreachable else-0.0: L1874 filters None and L1879 sort would TypeError first; silent coercion would mask invariant breaks.

finding.contract_claim: L1894-1895: In `_html_file_health_section`, rows are filtered at L1874 to exclude `r[1] is not None`, guaranteeing all remaining rows have non-None health. The `h_val = health if health is not None else 0.0` guard at L1895 is therefore unreachable as written.
finding.observed_behavior: Simplify to `h_val = health` since None values are already filtered out at L1874. Or keep for defensive coding clarity.

entries.adjudication_notes: Sole Path occurrence is the import: no runtime use, no annotations, no TYPE_CHECKING; no linter configured to catch F401.

finding.contract_claim: L25-25: `Path` is imported from `pathlib` (L25) but is never directly used anywhere in the file. All path construction uses `config.root_path / ...` where `config.root_path` is already a `Path` object — the `Path` constructor itself is never called.
finding.observed_behavior: Remove `Path` from the import: change `from pathlib import Path` to remove that name, or delete the import entirely if no other pathlib names are needed.

entries.adjudication_notes: TriageBatchResult declares exploration_traces with default_factory; sole return site always passes it. AttributeError impossible.

finding.contract_claim: L135-135: Accesses `result.exploration_traces` on the return value of `triage.decide_batch()`. If the batch result object does not have an `exploration_traces` attribute (e.g., in claim mode where no exploration traces are collected), this will raise AttributeError at runtime.
finding.observed_behavior: Verify that the `decide_batch` return type always includes `exploration_traces` (even as an empty list) for both modes, or guard with `getattr(result, 'exploration_traces', [])` and add a cross-file type annotation.

entries.adjudication_notes: Concept declares priority_score: float = 0.0; both live and deserialize paths compute it. Sort cannot AttributeError.

finding.contract_claim: L1031-1031: `c.priority_score` is accessed in `format_audit_report` when sorting underdocumented concepts, but `priority_score` is not visible as a standard field of `Concept` from this file. It may be set by `_compute_priority()` which is imported only in `_deserialize_doc_prompts`. If live-run `Concept` objects don't have this attribute, sorting will raise AttributeError.
finding.observed_behavior: Verify that `Concept` dataclass in `doc_prompts.py` always initializes `priority_score`, or that `_compute_priority` is called before any sort in the live code path.

entries.adjudication_notes: Missing import real but annotation is a string under __future__ annotations on a plain function param; repo has no annotation evaluator. Hygiene-only.

finding.contract_claim: L628-629: `Callable` is referenced in the string annotation `"Callable[[str, int, int], None] | None"` but is not imported anywhere in the file. With `from __future__ import annotations` active this causes no runtime error during normal execution. However, any call to `typing.get_type_hints(build_doc_prompts_async)` would raise a `NameError: name 'Callable' is not defined`.
finding.observed_behavior: Add `from collections.abc import Callable` (or `from typing import Callable`) to the imports, and remove the redundant inner quote wrapping since `from __future__ import annotations` already stringifies all annotations.

entries.adjudication_notes: BUILTIN_PROVIDER_MODELS is a static literal with all tiers for all five providers; .get() guard + never mutated. KeyError unreachable.

finding.contract_claim: L320-323: _prompt_model_defaults accesses defaults['small'], defaults['medium'], and defaults['large'] by hard-coded key without guarding against missing tiers. If any BUILTIN_PROVIDER_MODELS entry omits a tier, this raises KeyError.
finding.observed_behavior: Use defaults.get('small', '') or verify BUILTIN_PROVIDER_MODELS schema guarantees all three tiers for every provider entry.

entries.adjudication_notes: class_for_method maps every method, so ClassName( pattern added for all methods not just constructors; comment understates behavior feeding candidacy.

finding.contract_claim: L208-211: Comment says 'For constructors nested inside a class, also grep for ClassName(' but the code adds the ClassName( grep pattern for ALL methods whose function name appears in class_for_method — not only __init__/constructors. For non-constructor methods this adds a spurious constructor-call grep pattern, potentially introducing false call sites.
finding.observed_behavior: Change comment to 'For methods inside a class, also grep for ClassName( to catch constructor calls and direct class invocations' or restrict the pattern addition to only __init__-style constructors if that was the original intent.

entries.adjudication_notes: Stronger than claimed: adapter IS live (audit.py Phase 3 -> build_debris_claims -> finding_from_debris); docstring 'not wired / unit tests only, in this PR' factually false.

finding.contract_claim: L6-8: Module docstring contains PR-era language: 'It is **not** wired into `audit.py`'s live path in this PR — it is exercised by unit tests only'. The phrase 'in this PR' is a PR-submission artifact. Whether the adapter is now wired into audit.py's live path cannot be confirmed from this file alone.
finding.observed_behavior: Update to describe current wiring status without PR-relative language, e.g. 'It is not currently wired into `audit.py`'s live path' or confirm and remove/update if it has since been integrated.

entries.adjudication_notes: Comments accurately describe current best-effort behavior; finding is a feature request miscategorized as stale comment.

finding.contract_claim: L532-533: The `_PARSERS` dict maps `setup.cfg` and `Pipfile` to `_parse_requirements_txt` with comments noting the parser misses INI/TOML structure. These are accurate limitation notes, but `discover_manifests` includes both files via `_MANIFEST_FILES`, meaning callers will receive silently incomplete results for these formats without any warning at runtime.
finding.observed_behavior: Either implement proper parsers for `setup.cfg` (INI `[options] install_requires`) and `Pipfile` (TOML `[packages]`), or emit a warning when these manifests are parsed so callers know coverage is partial.

entries.adjudication_notes: Phase 1 never checks whether call sites pass params (all liveness judgment in Phase 2 LLM); docstring overstates Phase 1.

finding.contract_claim: L1-9: Module docstring states Phase 1 finds functions 'with callers but no call site passes those optional params.' Phase 1 does NOT check whether call sites pass optional params — it only collects call sites for candidates. The determination of whether params are actually passed is done exclusively in Phase 2 by the LLM. An AI agent reading this may incorrectly assume Phase 1 has filtering logic for param-passing.
finding.observed_behavior: Update Phase 1 description to: 'find exported functions with optional parameters that have callers — collecting all call sites for LLM verification in Phase 2.'

entries.adjudication_notes: Condition start <= prev_end+1 merges adjacent non-overlapping ranges; docstring claims only overlapping. One-line doc fix.

finding.contract_claim: L79-79: Docstring says 'Merge overlapping integer ranges' but the implementation also merges adjacent ranges (condition `start <= prev_end + 1` at L86, not `start <= prev_end`). Adjacent ranges like (1,5) and (6,10) are merged into (1,10) even though they do not overlap.
finding.observed_behavior: Update docstring to: 'Merge overlapping and adjacent integer ranges. Returns sorted, non-overlapping ranges.'

entries.adjudication_notes: SKILL.md directs agents to system prompts in tools.py; zero SYSTEM_PROMPT constants there - all 19 live in detector modules. Misdirects FP root-cause tracing.

finding.contract_claim: The skill instructs users to 'Read the system prompts and tool schemas in src/osoji/tools.py' when investigating false positives. While tool schemas are indeed in tools.py, the system prompts are located in the individual module files, not in tools.py. This misdirects investigators to the wrong file for critical context. [evidence: src\osoji\deadcode.py — "_DEAD_CODE_SYSTEM_PROMPT (L373–428) encodes the full decision logic for the LLM"]
finding.observed_behavior: Change the instruction to: 'Read the tool schemas in src/osoji/tools.py and the system prompts in the specific module that generates the finding (e.g., _DEAD_CODE_SYSTEM_PROMPT in deadcode.py, _ANALYZE_SYSTEM_PROMPT in doc_analysis.py, system prompts in junk_cicd.py, triage.py, plumbing.py, etc.)'

entries.adjudication_notes: Doc says observatory uses build_import_graph; it builds its own edge list; build_import_graph has no callers in src.

finding.contract_claim: The doc claims observatory 'Uses `build_import_graph` via `_build_import_graph_edges`' to emit the import graph. However, observatory's `_build_import_graph_edges` does NOT call `FactsDB.build_import_graph()`. It directly iterates `facts_db.all_files()` and resolves each import via `facts_db.resolve_import_source()`, building its own edge list with additional metadata (names, is_reexport) that the `build_import_graph()` method (which returns a simple `dict[str, set[str]]`) does not provide. [evidence: src\osoji\observatory.py — "_build_import_graph_edges (L152-176): Iterates facts_db.all_files(), skips entries where file_facts.classification is not None (doc files only), resolves each import via facts_db.resolve_import_source(). Deduplicates by (source, target) pair, accumulates names and propagates is_reexport = True if any import edge is a re-export."]
finding.observed_behavior: Change the observatory description to: 'Uses `all_files()` and `resolve_import_source()` via `_build_import_graph_edges` to emit the `import_graph` edge list in the export bundle.' This accurately reflects that observatory builds its own edge-list representation with metadata rather than delegating to `build_import_graph()`.

entries.adjudication_notes: Sweep skill Phase 1.5 endpoint resolution omits .osoji.local.toml which has highest TOML priority per push.py and CLAUDE.md.

finding.contract_claim: Phase 1.5 endpoint resolution omits .osoji.local.toml, which has higher priority than .osoji.toml in the actual push config merge chain. The skill instructs the agent to resolve the endpoint from (in priority order): OSOJI_ENDPOINT env var, .osoji.toml [push].endpoint, or ~/.config/osoji/config.toml [push].endpoint. However, push.py's _merge_push_config merges global → .osoji.toml → .osoji.local.toml with last-write-wins, meaning .osoji.local.toml has the highest TOML priority. If the endpoint is configured only in .osoji.local.toml, the agent following this skill would fail to find it for the display message. [evidence: src\osoji\push.py — "TOML config loaded via `_merge_push_config`: global config → `.osoji.toml` → `.osoji.local.toml` (last write wins per key)"]
finding.observed_behavior: Add .osoji.local.toml to the endpoint resolution list in Phase 1.5, placed at higher priority than .osoji.toml: 'OSOJI_ENDPOINT env var, .osoji.local.toml [push].endpoint, .osoji.toml [push].endpoint, or ~/.config/osoji/config.toml [push].endpoint'.

entries.adjudication_notes: CLAUDE.md Key architecture omits all six V1 modules (findings, evidence, triage, triage_exec, claim_builder, findings_adapter) which sit on the live Phase 3 path.

finding.contract_claim: The Key architecture section omits all V1 unified Finding/Triage architecture modules (claim_builder.py, evidence.py, findings.py, findings_adapter.py, triage.py, triage_exec.py) which are significant parts of the codebase as documented in the root shadow doc and individual module shadows. [evidence: _root — "`src/osoji/findings.py` + `evidence.py` + `triage.py` + `triage_exec.py` + `claim_builder.py` define the canonical v1 types"]
finding.observed_behavior: Add entries for the V1 architecture modules to the Key architecture list: findings.py (Unified Finding schema), evidence.py (Evidence schema), triage.py (Unified finding triage), triage_exec.py (Exploration tool executor), claim_builder.py (Debris claim builder), findings_adapter.py (Legacy-to-V1 finding adapter).

entries.adjudication_notes: LiteLLMProvider exists nowhere in src; retry/validation loop lives in DirectProvider.complete(). Doc references deleted class twice.

finding.contract_claim: The document references `LiteLLMProvider.complete()` twice as the location of the validation/retry loop. No `LiteLLMProvider` class exists in the codebase. The retry/validation loop is implemented in `DirectProvider.complete()` in `src/osoji/llm/_provider_base.py`. The `LiteLLMProvider` name appears to be from a prior architecture when litellm was used as the provider abstraction. All current providers (AnthropicProvider, ClaudeCodeProvider, GoogleProvider, OpenAIProvider, OpenRouterProvider) extend `DirectProvider`, which implements the shared tool-call retry and schema validation logic. [evidence: src\osoji\llm\_provider_base.py — "DirectProvider(LLMProvider) ... complete(messages, system, options) (L122-213) — main public entry point. Full async retry/validation loop"]
finding.observed_behavior: Replace both occurrences of `LiteLLMProvider.complete()` with `DirectProvider.complete()` (from `src/osoji/llm/_provider_base.py`). Also update any surrounding prose that implies a single LiteLLM-based provider to describe the DirectProvider base class used by all concrete SDK providers.
